metadata.name: ww-skeleton-loader
metadata.description: An animated placeholder block that indicates content is loading. Renders as a single `<div>` with a CSS animation applied — the element itself exposes only the animation type. Size, shape, and background color are controlled via standard style (width, height, border-radius, background-color). Intended to be rendered conditionally while data is loading, then swapped for the real content once `isLoading` is false.
features: 3 visually distinct animation styles: wave, pulse, shimmer
Zero runtime cost: no JS, just a `<div>` with a CSS animation
Shape fully controlled by style — use multiple skeletons to mimic lines / cards / avatars

properties.name: animationType
properties.description:
The animation applied to the placeholder. Visual descriptions:
- `pulse` — the whole box fades between ~60% and 100% opacity in a slow heartbeat (the most subtle / least distracting, good for dense lists)
- `wave` — a subtle translucent bar sweeps horizontally across the box every 1.5s (most 'skeleton-UI' feel, good for card placeholders)
- `shimmer` — a diagonal gradient light sweep from left to right every 2s (most premium / polished feel, good for hero/empty states)

specifications: The element renders an empty `<div>` — its width, height, border-radius, and background color MUST come from style (or design-system classes). Without a visible size and a background color, nothing is visible.

To toggle the skeleton on/off during loading, wrap in a v-if based on a loading variable you control — e.g. render `ww-skeleton-loader` when `isLoading`, render the real content when `!isLoading`. There is no built-in start/stop action.
`wave` and `shimmer` rely on a `::after` pseudo-element overlay and work best when the skeleton has a visible solid background color — pick a grey/light-grey to make the sweep visible.
`pulse` works on any background since it animates the opacity of the element itself; use it when you don't want the 'sweep' effect.

Common usage pattern: to mock a card, stack multiple skeletons — one tall rectangle (image), several short rectangles of decreasing width (title + body lines). Keep the skeleton layout close to the final content's layout so the swap feels seamless.
Set `border-radius` to `999px` for circular avatar placeholders, a small radius for text-line placeholders, a medium radius for card/image placeholders.

examples.description: A text-line skeleton placeholder bound to a loading flag. Style separately: width 100%, height 16px, radius 4px, bg grey-200.
A hero image placeholder with the shimmer animation (feels premium). Style separately: width 100%, height 240px, radius 12px, bg grey-200.
A dense list of skeleton items with the subtle pulse animation (less distracting than wave/shimmer when many are visible at once).
